html:
SCAP-R.BK / Srisawad Capital 1969

The best thesis is not the prior “hidden bank” story. SCAP is not a commercial bank and did not become CIMB Thai. It returned its finance license in 2022 and is now a non-bank retail lender focused on supervised personal loans, motorcycle hire purchase, and a new locked-phone hire-purchase product. That correction matters because the real opportunity is cleaner: the market is pricing SCAP as a busted motorcycle-finance book in runoff, while Q1 2026 suggests it may be turning into a smaller, better-underwritten, higher-return consumer credit platform.

At THB 1.20 on May 18, 2026, SCAP’s market cap is about THB 8.1 billion. Q1 2026 equity was THB 11.93 billion, so the stock trades at roughly 0.68x book. Q1 net profit was THB 420.8 million, up 136% year over year and 89% quarter over quarter, which annualizes to THB 1.68 billion or about THB 0.25 per share. That puts the stock on roughly 4.8x Q1 annualized earnings, despite Q1 still carrying elevated credit costs and a shrinking legacy loan book.

The key inflection is visible in the company’s own Q1 2026 MD&A. Loans fell 12.4% year over year to THB 25.6 billion because management has been deliberately tightening underwriting and reducing LTVs, yet profit surged. Interest income was down year over year, but up 6.6% quarter over quarter because the new mobile-phone hire-purchase business started contributing. Finance costs fell 29% year over year and 11% quarter over quarter as high-cost borrowings matured and were refinanced. Expected credit losses fell 21% year over year and 19% quarter over quarter. Losses on sales of foreclosed properties fell 46% year over year as used motorcycle collateral stabilized. Service and administrative expenses fell sharply after dealer-commission and workforce adjustments.

That is not a one-line accounting bounce. Revenue mix, funding cost, credit cost, operating cost, and collateral losses all moved in the right direction at the same time.

The market still sees SCAP as the 2022-2024 story: over-expanded motorcycle hire purchase, weak Thai household balance sheets, high dealer channel losses, shrinking receivables, and no obvious growth. That skepticism is rational but stale. The 2024 Opportunity Day presentation showed the old portfolio was 94% new motorcycle hire purchase, with Stage 3 loans already rising to 3.85% and Stage 2 at 8.31%. By Q1 2026, the company had taken the medicine: the loan book was cut, underwriting tightened, high LTV origination reduced, dealer incentives changed, and legacy high-cost liabilities paid down.

The new product is the part the market may be underestimating. SCAP says it has expanded mobile-phone hire purchase through a locked-phone system to more than 7,000 partner mobile-phone stores nationwide and wants to be a leader in that product within two years. This is not just “small-ticket gadget lending.” The locked-phone model changes collection leverage and loss severity: the asset is essential to the borrower, the ticket size is granular, the device can be controlled digitally, and distribution is broader than the older motorcycle dealer base. If the credit scoring works, SCAP can rebuild receivables without repeating the old motorcycle LTV mistake.

The forward economics are attractive even without heroic growth. Assume the loan book stays around THB 25-27 billion, interest income runs near Q1 levels, other income continues to benefit from recoveries, finance costs stay below the 2024 run rate, and credit costs remain elevated around 4.5-5.5% of loans. That still supports annual net income around THB 1.3-1.6 billion, or THB 0.19-0.24 per share. At THB 1.20, the stock is already discounting only a mediocre recovery. If credit costs normalize closer to 3.5-4.0% while phone hire purchase scales, earnings power can move toward THB 1.8-2.1 billion, or THB 0.27-0.31 per share.

The balance sheet is not bulletproof, but it is not distressed. Q1 assets were THB 28.4 billion, liabilities THB 16.5 billion, and equity THB 11.9 billion. Cash was THB 2.05 billion. Fitch’s Thailand coverage listed SCAP at A-(tha)/F2(tha) with a stable outlook, which supports access to refinancing at better cost than the market’s “broken lender” label implies. The company is also 72% owned by Srisawad Corporation, which reduces free-float liquidity but gives it group infrastructure, credit know-how, and funding credibility.

The retail setup is also good for torque. SET’s factsheet shows foreign shareholders at only 3.62% and NVDR ownership at just 0.22%, while the stock sits near penny-stock territory after falling from much higher levels in 2023-2024. There is no crowded foreign small-cap story here. It is a domestic Thai finance turnaround with low institutional attention and a visible quarterly inflection.

The main risk is that Thailand’s consumer credit cycle is still fragile. Household debt was about 86.7% of GDP at the end of 2025, and consumer repayment capacity remains pressured. SCAP’s NPLs still rose to THB 1.39 billion in Q1, or about 5.4% of loans, partly from responsible-lending repayment extensions and legacy origination. If the new locked-phone book grows too fast, it could recreate the exact credit problem investors are trying to underwrite away. Related-party control is another discount: SAWAD owns the company, minorities are along for the ride, and capital allocation has to be watched closely.

But the valuation gives room for those risks. A lender earning 7-8% ROE deserves a discount to book. A lender earning 12-15% ROE with stabilizing credit should not trade at 0.68x book and under 5x annualized earnings. Q1 2026 was the first hard evidence that SCAP may have crossed that line.

Bear case: THB 0.80, down 33%. Credit costs re-accelerate, NPLs keep rising, phone hire purchase proves low quality, and FY26 earnings fall back toward THB 600-700 million. The stock trades at 0.45x book.

Base case: THB 1.85, up 54%. FY26 earnings reach THB 1.4-1.6 billion, ROE moves into the low teens, and the market rerates SCAP to roughly 0.95x forward book or 8x normalized EPS.

Bull case: THB 2.70, up 125%. Locked-phone finance scales profitably, funding costs keep falling, ECL trends lower, and SCAP earns THB 1.9-2.1 billion. A 1.25-1.35x book multiple is reasonable for a cleaned-up, growing Thai consumer lender with mid-teens ROE.

Current price: THB 1.20. Base-case upside: about 54%. Bull-case upside: about 125%.
